news_coverage.query: Fire Burning in My Head ho

narrative:
Short impact summary

Published by Peter Lang Verlag in 2023, Fire Burning in My Head by David Y. F. Ho is an interdisciplinary scholarly work drawing on spirituality, psychoanalysis, aesthetics, and social psychology. While formal citation uptake remains nascent, the book has achieved modest library distribution and shows early signals of pedagogical relevance, situating it as an emerging contribution to humanistic and psychological inquiry.

Full narrative

At its intellectual core, Fire Burning in My Head occupies an ambitious interdisciplinary space, weaving together concepts from spirituality, psychoanalysis, aesthetics, dignity, and reverence. This thematic constellation signals a work concerned less with narrow disciplinary conventions than with the deeper architecture of human experience — the psyche understood not merely as clinical terrain but as a site of philosophical, sociological, and ethical significance. The book's engagement with epistemology and social psychology further suggests an author intent on interrogating how individuals come to know themselves and others within structures of meaning and value.

The book's pedagogical footprint, while still developing, is a noteworthy early signal. Ten mentions across open courseware environments indicate that educators are beginning to encounter and engage with the work's ideas in instructional contexts. Alongside holdings in three library collections, this pattern suggests the book is finding its way into the intellectual ecosystems of universities, even if formal citation networks have yet to register its influence. For a 2023 publication in a deeply humanistic register, this trajectory is consistent with the longer absorption cycles typical of philosophy- and psychology-adjacent monographs.

The work also carries a modest but meaningful connection to the United Nations Sustainable Development Goal of Reduced Inequalities, reflecting how its engagement with dignity, reverence, and the social dimensions of the psyche speaks to broader questions of human worth and equitable recognition. This linkage, while not the book's primary framing, underscores how philosophical and psychological scholarship can resonate with contemporary concerns about social justice and human flourishing.

Overall, Fire Burning in My Head represents a reflective, integrative scholarly endeavor from an author with an established presence in humanistic psychology. Its current citation and attention metrics place it in the early stages of academic reception — below the median for its cohort — but the nature of its inquiry, spanning the affective, the spiritual, and the sociological, positions it as a work whose impact may deepen gradually as readers across disciplines discover its arguments. Scholars working at the intersection of psychology, philosophy, and the study of human dignity will find it a substantive and thought-provoking contribution.
